title: On living near people whose ordinariness you envy
summary: Examine the experience of envying not another person's achievements or wealth, but the quieter texture of their daily life — and what that envy reveals about one's own conception of the good.
topic: The ethics and phenomenology of envying ordinary contentment
prompt: Write a 600–800-word personal-argumentative essay on the experience of envying another person's ordinary life rather than their exceptional achievements. Consider what this particular envy reveals about the values and assumptions you hold, and whether it points toward something you should examine or change. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid self-help resolution. Let the question remain productively open.
hintStarters: Rarely does envy arrive in the form we expect it — directed at wealth or fame. More often it attaches itself to…
What makes this variety of envy so difficult to discuss is not its intensity but its…
Granted, envy is widely regarded as a low emotion, unworthy of examination. Even so,…

sampleText:
Rarely does envy arrive in the form we expect it — directed at wealth or fame. More often it attaches itself to something smaller and harder to name: the particular ease with which a neighbour waters their garden on a Tuesday morning, the way a colleague talks about dinner with their family as though it were neither glamorous nor a disappointment but simply what happened. This is envy of ordinariness — and it is, I think, among the more revealing varieties of the emotion.

The standard taxonomy of envy divides it into malignant and benign forms. Malignant envy wishes the object of envy diminished; benign envy is emulative, drawing the envious person toward what they lack. Neither category captures the peculiar quality of envying quotidian contentment. What I envy in my neighbour is not something I can acquire through effort. I cannot train myself into a hedonic baseline I have apparently never had. The envy is not a prompt to action; it is closer to a diagnosis.

What makes this variety of envy so difficult to discuss is not its intensity but its apparent triviality. To confess that you envy a colleague's ordinary evening, their comfortable domesticity, their satisfaction in the pedestrian pleasures of a settled life, is to invite suspicion that you are either romanticising poverty of experience or indulging a theatrical form of dissatisfaction. Neither accusation is entirely unfair. There is a real risk that what one is actually envying is the idea of ordinariness — the unlived life projected onto someone who is, in reality, as complicated and restless as oneself.

And yet I do not think the envy is entirely self-delusion. It is pointing at something real: an appetite for intensity that has cost more than it produced. A life structured around the pursuit of exceptional experience — exceptional work, exceptional feeling, exceptional events — accumulates a kind of fatigue that the person watering their garden on a Tuesday does not seem to carry. It is this absence of ontological restlessness that constitutes the object of envy, not the activities themselves.

It is the diagnostic character of this emotion, not merely its discomfort, that deserves our attention. Envy tells us what we value in the negative: by registering the lack, it names a desire we have not fully acknowledged. When the desire is for more money or status, its message is at least legible, if not flattering. But when it is for the capacity to find sufficiency — to reach a threshold of sufficiency and rest there without ambition pulling forward — the message is more unsettling. It suggests that the restive ambition one has treated as identity may also be a kind of impoverishment.

Granted, envy is widely regarded as a low emotion, unworthy of sustained examination. Even so, to refuse to interrogate it is to leave a significant piece of self-knowledge on the floor. The person who envies ordinary contentment is not, I think, simply wishing for a quieter life; they are confronting the possibility that the framework within which they have organised their choices does not produce what they most deeply want. That confrontation is useful, even when — especially when — it does not resolve into a simple programme of change.

Disenchantment with one's own appetite for intensity is not the same as readiness to abandon it. The person envying the neighbour in the garden is rarely actually prepared to become that neighbour. What they are doing, perhaps, is conducting a form of invidious comparison that the philosopher Kierkegaard would have recognised: measuring not achievement against achievement, but mode of being against mode of being. And that comparison, however uncomfortable, is where the more interesting questions begin.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after a negative adverb for a confident opening
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Rarely does envy arrive in the form we expect it' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'envy' sau trạng từ phủ định 'Rarely'. Cấu trúc này không chỉ tạo tính trang trọng mà còn làm nổi bật ngay từ đầu rằng kinh nghiệm được mô tả đi ngược lại kỳ vọng thông thường.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Rarely does envy arrive in the form we expect it' inverts the auxiliary 'does' before the subject 'envy' after negative 'Rarely'. This both adds formality and immediately signals that the experience being described runs counter to expectation — the inversion enacts the surprise it announces.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to foreground the diagnostic value
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the diagnostic character of this emotion, not merely its discomfort, that deserves our attention' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is X, not merely Y, that Z' để đưa ra đặc tính chẩn đoán là điểm trọng tâm trong khi đồng thời bác bỏ sự khó chịu đơn thuần là không đủ lý do để chú ý. Đây là một động thái C1 chuẩn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the diagnostic character of this emotion, not merely its discomfort, that deserves our attention' is a cleft foregroundING the diagnostic dimension while simultaneously rejecting mere discomfort as sufficient reason to pay attention — 'not merely' embeds a concession inside the focus clause.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hiếm khi sự ghen tị đến theo hình thức chúng ta mong đợi — hướng đến sự giàu có hay danh tiếng. Thường thì nó gắn bó với thứ gì đó nhỏ hơn và khó đặt tên hơn: sự thoải mái đặc biệt mà một người hàng xóm tưới vườn vào sáng thứ Ba, cách một đồng nghiệp nói về bữa tối cùng gia đình như thể đó không phải là điều gì xa hoa cũng không phải là nỗi thất vọng mà chỉ đơn giản là những gì đã xảy ra. Đây là sự ghen tị với tính bình thường — và tôi nghĩ, đó là một trong những dạng cảm xúc tiết lộ nhiều hơn.

Phân loại thông thường về sự ghen tị chia nó thành hai dạng: độc hại và lành mạnh. Sự ghen tị độc hại muốn đối tượng bị ghen tị bị thu nhỏ; sự ghen tị lành mạnh có tính chất bắt chước, kéo người ghen tị đến gần những gì họ thiếu. Không loại nào nắm bắt được chất lượng đặc biệt của sự ghen tị với sự hài lòng thường nhật.

Chính tính chất chẩn đoán của cảm xúc này, không chỉ sự khó chịu của nó, mới xứng đáng được chú ý. Sự ghen tị cho chúng ta biết những gì chúng ta coi trọng theo cách phủ định: bằng cách ghi nhận sự thiếu hụt, nó đặt tên cho một ham muốn mà chúng ta chưa thừa nhận đầy đủ. Cứ cho là sự ghen tị thường bị coi là cảm xúc thấp hèn, không đáng kiểm tra lâu dài. Dù vậy, từ chối thẩm vấn nó là để lại một mảnh tự nhận thức quan trọng trên sàn.

Sự vỡ mộng với ham muốn cường độ của bản thân không giống với sự sẵn sàng từ bỏ nó. Người ghen tị với người hàng xóm trong vườn hiếm khi thực sự sẵn sàng trở thành người hàng xóm đó. Điều họ đang làm, có lẽ, là thực hiện một hình thức so sánh gây ác cảm mà triết gia Kierkegaard sẽ nhận ra: đo lường không phải thành tựu với thành tựu mà là phương thức tồn tại với phương thức tồn tại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion after a negative adverb?
options: Rarely envy arrives in the form we expect it.
Rarely does envy arrive in the form we expect it.
Envy rarely arrives in the expected form.
It is rarely that envy arrives in the expected form.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Rarely' at sentence start, the auxiliary 'does' must precede the subject 'envy' — 'Rarely does envy arrive…'

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the main subject of envy described in the essay?
options: Another person's professional success
Another person's wealth and social status
Another person's ordinary contentment and settled daily life
Another person's physical attractiveness
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay focuses on 'envy of ordinariness' — the neighbour's ease watering their garden, the colleague's unspectacular dinner — not wealth or achievement.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what happens on a Tuesday morning in the first paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why cannot this particular envy be resolved through effort, according to the essay?
options: Because it is illegal to envy one's neighbour
Because the object of envy — a hedonic baseline — is not acquirable through training or achievement
Because effort is incompatible with contentment
Because the envied person would object to being imitated
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says: 'I cannot train myself into a hedonic baseline I have apparently never had. The envy is not a prompt to action; it is closer to a diagnosis.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about training and the hedonic baseline.

mcQuestions.prompt: What risk does the essay acknowledge in envying another's ordinariness?
options: The risk of becoming too content and losing ambition
The risk that one is envying an idea of ordinariness projected onto someone equally complicated
The risk that the neighbour will notice the envy
The risk of developing malignant rather than benign envy
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay warns that 'what one is actually envying is the idea of ordinariness — the unlived life projected onto someone who is, in reality, as complicated and restless as oneself.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'unlived life projected onto someone.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'ontological restlessness' describe in the essay?
options: The physical restlessness that comes from too much coffee
A philosophical term for uncertainty about one's existence
The persistent existential dissatisfaction of a life organised around intensity and exceptional experience
The tiredness that comes from working too hard
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'ontological restlessness' to describe the fatigue of a life structured around exceptional experience — the very thing the contented gardener appears not to carry.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at what is absent in the person watering their garden.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say envy reveals about one's values?
options: It reveals that one secretly wishes to be less successful
It reveals what one values in the negative — naming a desire not yet fully acknowledged
It reveals a character flaw that should be corrected
It reveals that one has chosen the wrong career
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'Envy tells us what we value in the negative: by registering the lack, it names a desire we have not fully acknowledged.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence beginning 'Envy tells us what we value…'

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to emphasise what deserves attention?
options: The diagnostic character of this emotion deserves more attention than its discomfort.
It is the diagnostic character of this emotion, not merely its discomfort, that deserves our attention.
Both the diagnostic character and the discomfort of this emotion deserve our attention.
What deserves attention is this emotion's discomfort, not its diagnostic character.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not merely Y, that Z' is a cleft that foregrounds X while dismissing Y as insufficient — a C1 structure combining focus and embedded concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … not merely … that …' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay says that envying ordinariness suggests 'restive ambition may also be a kind of impoverishment'. What does it mean?
options: Ambitious people earn less money than contented people
An ambition that prevents reaching a threshold of sufficiency may deprive one of a deeper form of wellbeing
Restlessness causes physical poverty by preventing steady employment
Ambition and contentment are incompatible personality traits
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay suggests that ambition pulling one away from sufficiency might itself be a form of poverty — of a qualitative, existential kind — not financial.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'threshold of sufficiency' and what pulling away from it costs.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'diagnostic emotion' mean as used in the essay?
options: An emotion experienced by doctors
An emotion that, when examined, reveals something true about one's own values or condition
A clinical term for medically recognised emotional disorders
An emotion that reliably predicts future behaviour
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay treats the envy as a diagnostic in the medical sense — a symptom that, when interpreted, reveals the underlying condition of the patient's values.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what a doctor does with a diagnosis.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition correctly applies 'Granted… Even so…'?
options: Even so, envy is a low emotion. Granted, it is worth examining.
Granted, envy is widely regarded as a low emotion. Even so, to refuse to interrogate it is to leave significant self-knowledge on the floor.
Even so, self-knowledge is important. Granted, envy is low.
Granted and even so — both are important in this analysis.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' acknowledges the opposing position; 'Even so' pivots to the counter-argument. They must appear in this order.
mcQuestions.hint: The concession always precedes the pivot.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'benign envy' according to the essay?
options: Envy that is too mild to affect behaviour
Envy that draws the envious person toward what they lack by inspiring emulation
Envy that the envied person is unaware of
Envy directed only at close friends rather than strangers
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines benign envy as 'emulative, drawing the envious person toward what they lack' — motivating rather than destructive.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the word 'emulative' in the taxonomy paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say the essay ends without a programme of change?
options: Because the author is too lazy to recommend action
Because confronting the mismatch between one's framework and one's desires is valuable even without resolving into action
Because self-help advice is out of fashion
Because change is impossible for someone with restive ambition
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly says 'That confrontation is useful, even when — especially when — it does not resolve into a simple programme of change.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about confrontation being useful.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'unlived life' mean in the essay?
options: A life that ended prematurely
An imagined alternative life — the path not taken — projected onto someone whose life appears simpler
A philosophical concept about what counts as authentic living
A literary genre focusing on hypothetical events
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'unlived life' to describe the idealised alternative existence one projects onto the seemingly contented neighbour.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for how 'unlived life' is grammatically positioned in its sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'invidious comparison' mean at the essay's end?
options: A comparison that is invisible to those being compared
A comparison that creates resentment or unfairness by measuring incomparable things
A comparison made in a formal academic context
A comparison that reveals only positive qualities
mcQuestions.explanation: Invidious means likely to cause resentment or be seen as unfair. The essay uses it to describe comparing mode of being to mode of being — an uncomfortable but illuminating act.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'invidious' suggests: something likely to cause offence or resentment.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'threshold of sufficiency' describe in this essay?
options: The minimum income needed for a decent life
The point at which one has enough and can rest without ambition demanding more
A technical term in welfare economics for poverty measurement
The level of achievement required for social acceptance
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes envying 'the capacity to find sufficiency — to reach a threshold of sufficiency and rest there without ambition pulling forward.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what the threshold allows one to do after reaching it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever envied not someone's achievements but the quality of their everyday life? What specifically did you envy — and what does that tell you about your own values and choices? Does the essay convince you that examining envy is more useful than suppressing it?
